skill.name: azure-storage
skill.description: Guides Azure Storage work across Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake using Azure MCP, Azure CLI, and SDK quick references.
skill.summary: Use Azure Storage services with MCP commands, CLI fallbacks, SDK examples, tiers, redundancy, and lifecycle guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, network, dynamic import, and weak cryptography patterns. Review found these are markdown tables, fenced SDK examples, placeholder Azure service URLs, Microsoft Learn links, and substring false positives, with no evidence of prompt injection, malware, secret harvesting, or unauthorized exfiltration. The remaining risk is low because the skill includes user-mediated Azure CLI and SDK examples that can read or write Azure Storage resources when intentionally run.

low_findings.title: Azure CLI Examples Can Modify Storage Resources
low_findings.description: The CLI fallback includes az storage commands for listing, downloading, and uploading blobs. This is legitimate Azure Storage guidance, but users should review account, container, blob, and file parameters before running commands because upload and download operations affect cloud data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain real Azure CLI commands, including upload and download. They are documented examples with placeholders and no hidden execution path, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts are backticked package names, CLI snippets, Maven dependency blocks, or SDK code fences. I did not find evidence that the skill executes shell commands by itself or interpolates untrusted input into a command runner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear inside markdown tables and fenced documentation samples. No script file, command execution API, or automation wrapper was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static Network Alerts Are Azure Placeholders and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to Azure service endpoint templates and Microsoft Learn links. They do not send local files, credentials, environment variables, or user data to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are Azure Storage placeholder endpoints or official Microsoft documentation links. A search found no suspicious external domains or webhook-style exfiltration destinations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections cite lines about Azure Blob Storage descriptions, SDK reference text, and table partition key design. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or another weak algorithm being recommended or implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines discuss storage services, SDK links, and partitioning guidance, not cryptographic algorithms. Targeted search for weak crypto terms returned no supporting evidence.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alert Is a Go Import Block
low_findings.description: The dynamic import finding maps to a Go example import block in a fenced code sample. It is not a JavaScript dynamic import expression and does not load attacker-controlled code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is inside a documented Go quick start and uses a normal import block. There is no dynamic module path or runtime code loading behavior.

low_findings.title: Connection String Guidance Needs Care
low_findings.description: The file share references mention connection strings for simple setup. The same sections also recommend Entra ID or DefaultAzureCredential for production, so this is a minor documentation caution rather than a publication blocker.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Connection strings can contain secrets, but the guidance is framed as simple setup and balanced by production identity guidance. No secret values are embedded in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Mediated Azure Storage Write Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents az storage blob upload as a fallback command. This can write data to Azure Storage when a user intentionally supplies real account, container, blob, and file values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upload command is explicit and visible in documentation. It is not hidden, automatic, or connected to untrusted input handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated SDK Calls to Azure Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The SDK quick starts create authenticated clients for Azure Storage service endpoints. This is expected for the skill domain, but generated code should be reviewed before it is used against production resources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples authenticate with Azure SDK credentials and perform uploads. This is normal cloud SDK usage, with no evidence of unauthorized endpoints or secret exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Azure Storage choices and SDK details can slow cloud implementation work. This skill gives focused guidance for Blob Storage, Files, Queues, Tables, Data Lake, MCP commands, CLI fallbacks, and SDK setup.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake.
Lists Azure MCP storage commands for accounts, containers, blobs, downloads, and uploads.
Provides Azure CLI fallback commands for common storage account, container, and blob tasks.
Summarizes storage tiers, blob access tiers, and redundancy options.
Provides SDK package names and quick start examples for Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Rust, and .NET.
Links to focused SDK references for Blob, Queue, File Share, Data Lake, and Table workflows.
limitations: It does not authenticate to Azure or verify subscription permissions by itself.
It does not replace official Azure documentation for complete service limits and pricing.
Some SDK references are condensed and direct users to deeper package-specific skills for advanced patterns.
CLI and SDK examples require users to supply real account names, containers, paths, and credentials.

use_cases.title: Plan Storage Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare Azure Storage services, tiers, and redundancy options before choosing a design for application data.

use_cases.title: Operate Blob Workflows
use_cases.description: Find MCP or Azure CLI commands to list accounts, inspect containers, download blobs, and upload files.

use_cases.title: Start SDK Development
use_cases.description: Select package names and quick start patterns for storage clients in common programming languages.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Storage Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the right Azure Storage service for my workload. Ask for the data type, access pattern, performance needs, and retention requirements.

prompt_templates.title: List Blob Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the safest MCP command or Azure CLI fallback to list storage accounts, containers, and blobs for a selected Azure account.

prompt_templates.title: Build an SDK Quick Start
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a language-specific Azure Storage quick start for uploading a blob with identity-based authentication and clear placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production Storage Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure Storage plan for access tiers, redundancy, lifecycle management, identity, SDK usage, and operational risks.

output_examples.input: I need to store user-uploaded images and serve them from an app.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use Blob Storage with the hot tier for active content. Use lifecycle policies to move older images to cool or archive tiers when access drops.

output_examples.input: I need a quick way to inspect blobs in a container.
output_examples.output: Suggested result: list the storage account, list containers, then list blobs in the selected container. Confirm account and container names before running commands.

output_examples.input: I need Python code guidance for Storage Tables.
output_examples.output: Suggested result: use the Azure Data Tables package with DefaultAzureCredential. Design partition keys around query patterns and use parameterized queries.

best_practices: Prefer managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential for production authentication.
Confirm Azure account, container, path, and overwrite settings before running write commands.
Choose access tiers, redundancy, and lifecycle rules based on measured access patterns.
anti_patterns: Do not paste storage account keys or connection strings into prompts or shared files.
Do not use archive tier for data that needs immediate reads.
Do not run upload, download, or delete commands against production resources without reviewing parameters.

faq.question: Does this skill manage Azure resources directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, MCP command names, CLI examples, and SDK references. Users still control authentication and execution.

faq.question: Which Azure Storage services does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Blob Storage, File Shares, Queue Storage, Table Storage, and Data Lake Storage Gen2.

faq.question: Does it support multiple programming languages?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Rust, and .NET package or quick start guidance.

faq.question: Can it help choose storage tiers?
faq.answer: Yes. It summarizes standard and premium accounts, blob hot, cool, cold, and archive tiers, and redundancy choices.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production data?
faq.answer: Use it carefully. Review all generated commands and code before running them against production Azure accounts.

faq.question: Does it include pricing details?
faq.answer: No. It gives architectural guidance, but users should check current Azure pricing and regional service limits separately.
